category_title: Students who talk out to access peer attention
scenario_title: Independent work time talking-out for peer attention
minimal_context: Craig is in the general classroom during independent work time, expected to complete his assigned tasks.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: It is time for independent work. Please start working quietly on your assigned tasks.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Turns to a peer) Hey, did you see the game last night?

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Craig, remember the rules for independent work time.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: I am just asking a question. (Turns back to peer) Seriously though, what did you think of that last play?

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Please focus on your own assignment.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (Laughs as peer responds back) I know, right? It was crazy! Look at this drawing I did of it.

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: It is time for independent work. Please start working quietly on your assigned tasks.

STUDENT: (Turns to a peer) Hey, did you see the game last night?

TUTOR: Craig, remember the rules for independent work time.

STUDENT: I am just asking a question. (Turns back to peer) Seriously though, what did you think of that last play?

TUTOR: Please focus on your own assignment.

STUDENT: (Laughs as peer responds back) I know, right? It was crazy! Look at this drawing I did of it.

context.environment: General classroom setting during independent work time
context.materials: Assigned tasks, business-size appointment cards with checklists, color-coded signs (green and red) placed in classroom zones

challenging_behavior_trigger.observable_behavior: Repeatedly engage in disruptive off-task and talking-out behaviors, making verbal comments or questions unrelated to the task

behavior_patterns.observable_behavior: Results in a persistent pattern of talk outs and contingent access to peer interactions
